title: Query active incidents using GlideRecord
question: Write a background script to query active incidents and print the incident number and short description.
summary: Queries active incidents with GlideRecord from Scripts - Background.
exactAnswer: Use GlideRecord on the incident table, add an active=true condition, execute query(), and loop with next() to print the fields with gs.print.
steps: Open All > System Definition > Scripts - Background.
Paste the script into the editor.
Run the script.
Review the output section for incident number and short description.
commonMistakes: Forgetting to filter active records when the requirement asks for active only.
explanation: GlideRecord initializes the incident table query.
gs.print writes readable output in Scripts - Background.

title: Query P1 incidents created in the last 24 hours
question: Write a background script to print active P1 incidents created in the last 24 hours.
summary: Uses encoded query conditions for a common ITSM interview script.
exactAnswer: Use GlideRecord on incident with active=true, priority=1, and sys_created_on within the last 24 hours. Then print number, caller, and short description.
steps: Open Scripts - Background.
Paste the script.
Run it and inspect the output list.
expectedOutput: INC0010456 | Abel Tuter | Major outage in payroll app
commonMistakes: Using getValue for caller_id when a readable display value is expected.
Using impact/urgency filters instead of priority when the requirement explicitly says P1.
explanation: Encoded queries make relative-date conditions compact.
orderByDesc shows the newest records first.

title: Count open incidents by assignment group with GlideAggregate
question: Write a script to show the number of active incidents for each assignment group.
summary: Uses GlideAggregate for grouped counts instead of looping every record.
exactAnswer: Use GlideAggregate on incident, filter to active records, group by assignment_group, and print the group name with the aggregate count.
steps: Open Scripts - Background.
Paste and execute the script.
Review grouped counts in the output.
commonMistakes: Using GlideRecord when only grouped counts are needed.
explanation: groupBy creates one output row per assignment group.

title: Scheduled job to auto-close resolved incidents after 7 days
question: Write a scheduled script execution that closes incidents in Resolved state when they have been resolved for more than 7 days.
summary: Common support automation scenario using GlideRecord and date filtering.
exactAnswer: Use a scheduled script execution that finds incidents with state Resolved and resolved_at older than 7 days, sets the state to Closed, and updates a closure note if required by the process.
steps: Open System Definition > Scheduled Jobs > New.
Choose Scheduled Script Execution.
Set the frequency and paste the script.
Test in a sub-production instance before scheduling in production.
commonMistakes: Hardcoding state numbers without confirming the instance values.
Using current in a scheduled job script where GlideRecord is required.
explanation: Scheduled jobs run server-side without form context.
Use process-approved state values and field names for your instance.

title: ACL script to restrict sensitive incidents
question: Write a read ACL script that only allows users with a specific role or members of the assignment group to read sensitive incidents.
summary: Server-side ACL script example with role and group logic.
exactAnswer: Use an ACL script that first checks a privileged role and otherwise validates whether the current user is a member of the incident's assignment group. Return true only when one of those checks passes.
steps: Create a read ACL on the incident table or target field.
Add a condition if needed to target only sensitive records.
Paste the script and test with impersonation.
commonMistakes: Using gs.hasRole('admin') as the only security design instead of creating a dedicated role.
Forgetting to test with impersonation using users who are and are not group members.
explanation: ACL scripts should be efficient because they run frequently.
Dedicated roles are clearer than overusing admin logic.

title: Fix script to deactivate inactive users based on a custom flag
question: Write a fix script that deactivates sys_user records where u_disable_account is true and the user is still active.
summary: Example of controlled data correction using a fix script.
exactAnswer: Use GlideRecord on sys_user, filter to active users where the custom field is true, set active=false, and update each record. Log the number of records changed.
steps: Create a fix script record.
Paste the script and review in sub-production first.
Run and confirm the count in the output.
commonMistakes: Running mass user updates directly in production without validation.
Forgetting to constrain the query to only target intended records.
explanation: Fix scripts should be controlled and auditable.

title: Query CMDB relationships for a CI
question: Write a background script to print all parent and child relationships for a CI.
exactAnswer: Use cmdb_rel_ci, query records where either parent or child equals the target CI sys_id, and print the parent, relationship type, and child display values.
steps: Find the target CI sys_id.
Open Scripts - Background.
Paste the script with the CI sys_id.
Run and review the printed relationship list.
commonMistakes: Forgetting that relationship type is stored separately and has meaning.
Using a plain reference field instead of cmdb_rel_ci for dependency analysis.
explanation: cmdb_rel_ci is the relationship table used for dependency modeling.

title: Bulk update without triggering business rules using setWorkflow(false)
question: Write a script that updates a flag on old incidents while suppressing business rules and engines.
exactAnswer: Use GlideRecord to query the target records, call setWorkflow(false) before update to suppress business rules and engines, and then update the field carefully after validating the impact.
steps: Open Scripts - Background in a non-production environment first.
Paste the script and verify the query scope.
Run after change approval if required.
commonMistakes: Using setWorkflow(false) casually in business process scripts without understanding downstream impact.
Failing to constrain the query tightly before mass updates.
explanation: This pattern is for controlled maintenance, not everyday transaction logic.
